title: The 26th Mentoring Talk by Prof. Zhenan Bao of Stanford ...
text:
thank you all
good afternoon to all those attending in
our local time zone
and good morning good evening to
everyone else tuning in from abroad
i'm truly thrilled ladies and gentlemen
to welcome you to this very fine
addition to our series of mentoring
talks
we are very honored to host here today
one of the foremost women in academia
professor ginambao for our 26th
mentoring talk
the first mentoring talk for this
academic year
during these dire conditions in lebanon
our distinguished guest is here today to
light a candle in our darkness
our esteemed guest
is a holder of many prestigious
accolades
professor bau
is among the top most ever sighted
female researchers
her current age index is 192
with almost 140 000 citations
she is the chairperson of the chemical
engineering department at stanford
university
even though professor bao has always
been a very modest person
she always dreams big
and keeps looking for new challenges
she believes that the research she is
working on which involves mimicking the
human the human skin to design
electronic devices
that are stretchable
self-healing and homogeneously effective
will reach its peak in the next 10 to 20
years
professor bao aims to use these devices
to transform the world and provide a new
generation of medical devices
yet
we are not inviting professor bao to
talk about her grandiose research
accomplishments but rather we ask her to
shed light on her failures and obstacles
professor bao was born
and raised in engine
china
she grew up in a household of scientists
in which her father broadened her
curiosity regarding scientific questions
of the universe at a very early age of
three years old
he asked her whether a popsicle would
float or sink in the lake
and she believed it would sink
so her father asked her to throw her
popsicle in the lake to test her
anticipation and rather than sinking the
popsicle floated
this experiment has taught her the first
concept of science
density
professor baugh believes that it was
this incident that inspired her to
become what she is today and what she
aspires to be in the future
her family immigrated during her
undergraduate studies into a modest
house in the united states
to provide her with a better education
she used to work multiple jobs from
working at the factory
to working part-time at the supermarket
alongside her education to support her
parents
being a mother of two beautiful kids
she dedicates all her spare time to her
children and enjoys hiking and cooking
with them
as a greatly established researcher
professor bao believes that the role of
women in stem should be encouraged by
raising awareness and implementing
policies that ensure gender equity
without further ado i shall now leave
the floor entirely to the professor of
the hour
ginabao so we so we may all learn
together how she has moved through
hardship to the stars
professor baum the floor is yours
thanks uh so much professor kaffarani
and thanks so much for the invitation
to to share a little bit about my
own experience
uh kind of coming a long way from um
from china to u.s
and uh then
starting my own academic career
so so my my plan is to um
to to
start with uh a little bit
of some slides that
helps to
guide my talk and
then i'll open up to uh any questions uh
uh you might have and uh have um
a conversation with you uh so uh i would
love to to hear from you and your
questions
uh as um uh professor
kavarani already mentioned that
i grew up in china
and uh this is a kind of a brief
timeline of
the important things that happened uh in
my uh career and in my life uh i grew up
in china and then came to the united
states um when i was in college in
1990 some of you probably were not even
born
at that time
that's the time when china just started
to open up to the western world and
my grandma
has been living in the u.s so we
immigrated
at the time and then
i worked in factory supermarket
for a year or so to support myself
and finished
college and then went to university of
chicago to
study graduate school and then went to
bowel laboratories um
worked there for eight years and then
stanford
and i got my phd in 1995
in bell labs became a distinguished
member of technical staff in early 2000
then in stanford they don't hire people
who
[Music]
who has not had
any teaching experience so
even though i have been out of phd for a

after that
a few years ago i became department
chair actually i just finished my term

now
along the way i started
two companies and also established a
initiative in stanford so my plan is to
um

so my plan is to tell you a little bit

so grew up um
in china nanjing china actually it's
kind of in the middle of china very
close to shanghai now with the fast

or coming out of their nest

that's unique to that region and
i've always fascinated with
these stone and
uh and this is what you would be able to

in the sand in where i play in the

places so we go hiking there are little

where i can find the fractions of stone

great pleasure when i
when i discover a small piece and then
that made me imagine
how beautiful they could be
if i get the uh the entire intact piece
so that that
joy of being able to really
look through nature or look through um

but um at that time also um
as part of my
college study
i already started to
receive mentorship and mentorship has
been
tremendously important uh for for my uh

career uh this is uh professor chishua

is a well-known polymer physicist

when i was in college
he gave me the opportunity to do a

at the time i
did not know that much about polymers

what i remember most was that i was able
to have a material it was kind of gooey

but i was able to hold it in my hand and

and uh professor uh
shia was really the person who
who introduced me to a polymer field

1990 i immigrated to the united states

so this is i think somewhere
somewhere
my arrow is pointing is where uh the uh

to receive a

and uh just the the
the very high level of
of uh
scholarship uh even on the campus uh so

in chicago
who started doing research
on polymer chemistry
and
i went to chicago actually
mainly because my relatives were in

situation at that time

that was an easy choice i just went

plus i really enjoyed polymer work when

to join professor lupinu's group
so in his group uh my work was related
to
investigating actually it's all about

the organic chemistry

but i was put in there so i started

my project was to investigate uh

right now these reactions are very

but back then uh
uh more than 20 years ago it was

and what i want to pull your attention

uh actually uh this is uh uh two years
after i joined lupin's group
you see that i was very fortunate have
three publications
in in my
second year

this was because
actually when i joined lupin's group i

the
apparatus to use i have to first

but lupine was able to as a
a great mentor he was able to choose

to
get started to to really learn about

and that was instrumental in helping me

he wrote the first paper here you see
his
name was first because he actually wrote

was under his guidance that did

this was my first paper and then i

so this
was really
this productivity at the beginning was

and then the other thing i want to point

take x-ray diffraction

people have never observed that

but we did not have any of this

there wasn't any shared facility
so lupine found out in the geophysics

there is an x-ray diffraction so he

core

but of course the afm that time was

first take it and then it's showing um

that experience taught me to
to reach out to others

i was actually
graduating before

even though i didn't have any research

because

in science and

and uh there

for two weeks and

modern microelectronics was invented

basically all the

to add the skin functionality such as

really important enabling discovery was

high degree of disorder in these

that improves the charge transport but

the number of units that going from a

our first version was able to

the sensors we invent and then circuits

with the development of these
